description:
You are working on a small Kubernetes Operator project placed in the user’s home directory.

Project layout that already exists before you start:
- /home/user/operator-manifests/deployment.yaml
- /home/user/operator-manifests/service.yaml

Both YAMLs contain valid Kubernetes resources (they are small; you do NOT need to create or modify them).

Your objectives (all steps must succeed ― automated tests will inspect the results exactly as described):

1. Create a new directory strictly at  
   /home/user/operator-docs  

2. Inside the new directory generate a Markdown file named  
   /home/user/operator-docs/operator-manifests.md  
   The file MUST match **all** of the following rules exactly (the tests are case-, space- and pipe-character sensitive):

   2.1  First line is an H1 heading:  
        # Operator Manifests Documentation

   2.2  Second line is completely blank (no spaces, only “\n”).

   2.3  Starting on the third line comes a GitHub-flavoured Markdown table with the **header row shown below verbatim**:  
        | File | Kind | Name | Namespace | ApiVersion |

        • The next line must be the separator line  
          |------|------|------|-----------|------------|

        • After the separator line, add ONE row per YAML found directly inside /home/user/operator-manifests (do not recurse into sub-dirs).  
          The row format is:

          | operator-manifests/᠎<filename> | <Kind> | <metadata.name> | <metadata.namespace> | <apiVersion> |

          The file column must be the **relative** path exactly as shown above (prefix “operator-manifests/”).  
          Rows must be sorted in ASCII order by the relative file path.  
          Use the values read from each YAML. No additional columns, pipes or spaces at line ends.

   2.4  After the last table row place **one blank line**, then append a line starting with  
        Last generated:  
        followed by the current date in ISO-8601 format (YYYY-MM-DD).  
        Example →  Last generated: 2024-05-02

3. Place a Markdown-lint configuration file at  
   /home/user/.markdownlint.yaml  
   with exactly the following YAML contents (no more, no less, correct indentation):

   ---
   default: true
   MD013:  
     line_length: 120  
     tables: false

   This keeps the default rules but sets MD013 line-length to 120 and disables it for tables.

4. Lint every Markdown file that resides anywhere under /home/user (only ordinary files, follow symlinks if any) using markdownlint CLI and write the lint output into  

   /home/user/operator-docs/lint_report.log

   Required log format:

   • If linting finds **zero** problems the log must contain **exactly one line** (ending with “\n”):  
     PASS: all markdown files pass linting

   • If problems are found, write one separate line per violation in the exact format used by markdownlint-cli:  
     <relative/path/to/file>:<line> <rule> <short-description>  
     (The automated tests will reject any other format.)

5. Make sure the shell exit status of the linting command is 0 when the PASS line is produced and non-zero when violations are present. (Tests will indirectly confirm this through the content of lint_report.log.)

Hints (NOT checked by the tests):
• You may write a small shell or Python script to parse YAML (grep + sed/awk is fine; full YAML parsing not required because the manifests are simple).  
• markdownlint-cli is pre-installed in the execution container as the command “markdownlint”.  
• Keep lines inside the generated Markdown within 120 characters so the linter stays quiet.

Deliverables that the checker will look for:
- /home/user/operator-docs/            (directory, exists)
- /home/user/operator-docs/operator-manifests.md  (Markdown file, exact format)
- /home/user/.markdownlint.yaml         (config file, exact content)
- /home/user/operator-docs/lint_report.log        (log file, correct single-line PASS or detailed violations)

Follow the specifications rigorously; small deviations in spacing, capitalization, table alignment or file paths will make automated tests fail.
truth:
Initial files already present before the task starts:

1. /home/user/operator-manifests/deployment.yaml  
   Content (with trailing newline):
   ---
   apiVersion: apps/v1
   kind: Deployment
   metadata:
     name: sample-deployment
     namespace: default

2. /home/user/operator-manifests/service.yaml  
   Content (with trailing newline):
   ---
   apiVersion: v1
   kind: Service
   metadata:
     name: sample-service
     namespace: default

Expected artefacts after correct task completion:

A. /home/user/operator-docs/operator-manifests.md  
   The automated test will assert the following lines **exactly** (where <DATE> is the ISO-8601 date of generation, matched with a regex “\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}”):

   Line 1: # Operator Manifests Documentation
   Line 2: (blank)
   Line 3: | File | Kind | Name | Namespace | ApiVersion |
   Line 4: |------|------|------|-----------|------------|
   Line 5: | operator-manifests/deployment.yaml | Deployment | sample-deployment | default | apps/v1 |
   Line 6: | operator-manifests/service.yaml | Service | sample-service | default | v1 |
   Line 7: (blank)
   Line 8: Last generated: <DATE>

   The test does not hard-code the date but verifies the pattern.

B. /home/user/.markdownlint.yaml must contain exactly:

   ---
   default: true
   MD013:
     line_length: 120
     tables: false

C. /home/user/operator-docs/lint_report.log  
   The happy-path test expects the single line:
   PASS: all markdown files pass linting

The automated checker will:
1. Verify that /home/user/operator-docs exists and is a directory.
2. Validate the exact lines 1-6 and regex for line 8 in operator-manifests.md.
3. Confirm that .markdownlint.yaml matches the required YAML block literally.
4. Read lint_report.log; it must have exactly one non-empty line and must match  
   ^PASS: all markdown files pass linting\n$
5. Ensure no other files under /home/user/operator-docs/ are modified other than those described.

If any of these conditions fail, the task is considered unsuccessful.
